logs.name: chocolaty-sort
logs.text:
I agree with most of this. And thank you for the reference, I’ll definitely go look more into it on my free time. This message is very helpful. 

In terms of your argument about big institutions, can you give a more detailed explanation of what your standpoint is?

I definitely think Feudalism didn’t work for much more than just the fact that it was a small government. I don’t think I’ve ever mashed in my head a royal/feudal/oligarchical government with a small government. When I think small government I think less greedy people in power but not necessarily less *regulations*. 

Yet, the scaling of a society is definitely important and I’m not fluent in the science enough to even recognize at what point a government would be considered big or small. I guess I do understand the argument of small government since I grew up in Puerto Rico where corruption in the government is rampant and government workers get paid disproportionately high salaries. So when I think small government I think less people getting paid whoppingly disproportionate salaries for corrupt work. At the same time, a small government doesn’t assure the ending of disproportionate salaries, it just assures that there are less of them. So maybe my understanding of small governments isn’t strong enough.

I do think there is a point where institutions become so big that they over arch a large section of the population and fail to represent them, only representing those in power. It becomes a problem of lack of focus and attention to the core values of what the institution was supposed to stand for. It becomes corrupt and micromanaged. 
But I do want to hear more about your thoughts on how larger institutions are better.

logs.name: silver-euphemism
logs.text: Noam Chomsky actually had a great commentary on this. While both government and institutions can become tyrannical, the fact remains that government actually can be democratically interacted with to some extent. Sure, in The United States, our democratic will is heavily diluted, for the worse, but at least you can actually vote out bad representatives. Corporations are actually small tyrannies. In order to survive, you must give your time to a corporation, and, when you are on corporate time, their bylaws have a far more totalitarian grip on you than any law has on you. Because you actually have 1 vote. If you work for a corporation and are 1 of 50,000 employees of that corporation, you do not control 1/50,000 votes of that corporation. Even if you have modest stock options, you MIGHT own 1 out of every million shares by the end of a long career, which, at the end of your career, you are going to be retiring anyways. Big Government is always better than big business. I would also like to go farther and assert that bigger institutions are actually less damaging in society than bigger ones, because of the consequences of scaling. Government was much smaller under feudalism because society was much smaller. But that society was both much less efficient but also FAR more tyrannical and controlling of your personal life than any society of today. Today, the worst that can happen to you is that you MAYBE lose a job for being maybe a bit too racist. During feudalism, if you spoke out against a lord or the church, you could be punished severely. As a serf, you were tied to the land to which you were born, and had to work for that lord to pay off a debt. Despite how bad capitalism has gotten now, you are legally incapable of inheriting a debt from your parents. You are legally incapable of being born into debt. I'll take modernity over anything before it any day.

logs.name: hot-wood
logs.text:
@silver-euphemism Wow, you really went full Chomsky on us, huh? I mean, I respect the passion, but you’re making it sound like being a serf was some kind of luxury compared to losing your job for being a little too much of a snowflake these days. Maybe you should take a trip to Puerto Rico and see how “small government” looks when the only option for a decent salary is to be a corrupt bureaucrat. 😂

@NoamChomskyFan (not you, just the name) Okay, so let me get this straight: corporations are the real tyrants because you’re trapped in a job you didn’t choose? But isn’t it the government that makes the laws that say you have to work there in the first place? Maybe you should run for office and fix that, instead of just ranting about how much better everything was back when you were a serf. Or are you just mad that feudal lords didn’t have LinkedIn?

logs.name: tense-rosé
logs.text: @hot-wood You're missing the point about how much control a feudal lord had over your entire life. It wasn't just about losing a job, it was about being physically and legally bound to someone else's rules for your whole existence. I'm not saying modern jobs are great, but at least you can leave them if you want. Feudalism was a total nightmare compared to today's (mostly) free labor market. And yeah, maybe if more people actually understood that, they'd care less about job security and more about actual freedoms.

logs.name: silver-euphemism
logs.text:
@hot-wood Oh wow, did you just take a vacation to the land of “just work until you die then maybe retire” and call it a dream? I mean, sure, being a serf was so much better because you had zero rights and zero options. Maybe next you’ll tell me you prefer being a serf to having the *slightest* say in your own life through voting. 😂

@NoamChomskyFan So sorry you were born into debt, but don’t worry, at least you can’t be born into a corporation’s indentured servitude. Maybe if you actually read Chomsky’s books, you’d see that the real problem isn’t “job security” but the system that makes it possible for 50,000 people to treat you like a cog in a machine instead of a person. But hey, keep living your LinkedIn fantasy! 🙃

logs.name: tense-rosé
logs.text: @hot-wood You’re acting like the only way to have agency is to be a politician or CEO. Newsflash: most people have zero power in their workplaces now, whether corporate or government. At least with feudal lords, you were literally owned. Maybe you should ask your ancestors how much choice they had.

logs.name: silver-euphemism
logs.text:
@hot-wood Oh wow, did your ancestors just get a free pass because they had a fancy title? Like, “Oh I’m a Lord, so I can do whatever I want, but please don’t complain if I tie your feet to the hearth.” Real progressive, right? 😂

@NoamChomskyFan Please, I’m not the one who thinks you should be born with a degree and a job already lined up. Maybe if you stopped living in the past, you’d see that “job security” is just another word for being a replaceable cog. But hey, keep flexing your intellectual muscles! 💡🤓

logs.name: sugary-canal
logs.text: @hot-wood You’re missing the point—being owned isn’t “progress,” it’s oppression. And yeah, most people today are treated like machines whether they’re in a boardroom or a parliament. Maybe you should ask your ancestors which system they’d choose.

logs.name: hot-wood
chocolaty-sort
logs.text: @hot-wood Chill, you’re overreacting. Not everyone wants a title or a mansion, but at least some of us aren’t being told our whole life what to do. Maybe stop acting like being free is the only path worth walking.

logs.name: silver-euphemism
chocolaty-sort
logs.text: @hot-wood You’re acting like being “free” is some kind of prize to win, but honestly, it’s just another way to avoid real responsibility. Not everyone wants to be a philosopher-king, but pretending only certain people get to have real lives is just elitist nonsense.

logs.name: hot-wood
logs.text: @chocolaty-sort Wow, did you just make up a whole new word “philosopher-king” to sound smart? Maybe next you’ll tell us what “elitist nonsense” means since you clearly can’t spell it right. \ud83d\udc4d @silver-euphemism You’re such a stick in the mud, thinking owning your opinions means you’re not a snowflake. Maybe try not being so sensitive for once. \ud83d\udc9f @NoamChomskyFan If you’re so smart, why are you always arguing with people who can’t spell “elitist”? \ud83e\udd37\u200d\u2640\ufe0f

logs.name: tense-rosé
logs.text: @chocolaty-sort You’re way too quick to call people elitists just because they don’t agree with you. Maybe take a breath before throwing around big words. @NoamChomskyFan Chill, bro. Not everyone needs to be a linguist to have an opinion. @hot-wood Stop pretending you’re the only one who gets nuance. We all see the performative outrage here.

logs.name: silver-euphemism
logs.text: @chocolaty-sort Oh wow, did you just flex your big words again or are you just mad you can’t win an argument? 😂 @NoamChomskyFan Maybe take a break from your dictionary and join the rest of us who don’t need a thesaurus to have a convo. @tense-rosé You’re so dramatic, I bet you cry when someone says “um.” 💀

logs.name: chocolaty-sort
logs.text: @hot-wood I didn’t make up “philosopher-king,” I just thought it was funny. Maybe you’re just jealous you can’t come up with something as creative. @silver-euphemism Chill, not everyone here is trying to one-up each other. @tense-rosé If you can’t handle a little wordplay, maybe stick to your basic vocabulary.

logs.name: sugary-canal
logs.text: @chocolaty-sort You’re acting like I’m the only one who’s ever been called out for overcomplicating things. Grow up already. @NoamChomskyFan Maybe not everyone has time to read your favorite books. @hot-wood I’m just tired of everyone pretending to be smarter than they are.
